verses.text: And the Ziphites came unto Saul to Gibeah, saying, Doth not David hide himself in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon? Guidance: The Ziphites are back. The same informants from chapter 23 trek up to Gibeah again with the same kind of tip-off, and the same location even — 'the hill of Hachilah, before Jeshimon.' The verse is its own indictment of how little has changed despite the En-gedi cave reconciliation in chapter 24. Saul wept publicly. Saul confessed. Saul acknowledged David would be king. And then he went home, and within months the same informants are at his door with the same information, and the same king is mustering. The chapter is teaching us that emotional confession without changed conduct produces the same outcome the next time the temptation arrives. The chapter is essentially chapter 24 repeated, with different geography, to make the point that Saul's repentance in the cave was not a turning. Some apologies are weather, not climate.

Then Saul arose, and went down to the wilderness of Ziph, having three thousand chosen men of Israel with him, to seek David in the wilderness of Ziph. And Saul pitched in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way. But David abode in the wilderness, and he saw that Saul came after him into the wilderness. Guidance: The exact composition repeats: Saul rises, three thousand chosen men, the same hill, the same trail, the same campsite location. The repetition is the narrator's quiet sigh — we have already read this in chapter 24. The shift is in the verbs: Saul 'pitched in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way' — he sets up his camp on the road, in fixed position, the way an army intends to hold ground while patrols sweep. David, 'in the wilderness,' simply sees the army arrive. David's information network is excellent; he has scouts who tell him about Saul's movements without Saul ever knowing the watching is happening. The man under pursuit becomes a master of observation; the man pursuing becomes increasingly visible and increasingly unable to see who is watching him. The narrative compass is shifting; David is becoming the one with the higher viewpoint.

Then Saul arose, and went down to the wilderness of Ziph, having three thousand chosen men of Israel with him, to seek David in the wilderness of Ziph. And Saul pitched in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way. But David abode in the wilderness, and he saw that Saul came after him into the wilderness. Guidance: The exact composition repeats: Saul rises, three thousand chosen men, the same hill, the same trail, the same campsite location. The repetition is the narrator's quiet sigh — we have already read this in chapter 24. The shift is in the verbs: Saul 'pitched in the hill of Hachilah, which is before Jeshimon, by the way' — he sets up his camp on the road, in fixed position, the way an army intends to hold ground while patrols sweep. David, 'in the wilderness,' simply sees the army arrive. David's information network is excellent; he has scouts who tell him about Saul's movements without Saul ever knowing the watching is happening. The man under pursuit becomes a master of observation; the man pursuing becomes increasingly visible and increasingly unable to see who is watching him. The narrative compass is shifting; David is becoming the one with the higher viewpoint.

David therefore sent out spies, and understood that Saul was come in very deed. Guidance: David verifies the report through his own intelligence apparatus. The verse is short but operationally significant: David does not rely on hearsay, does not assume the worst. He sends spies and 'understood that Saul was come in very deed.' The 'in very deed' (Hebrew el nakon, 'with certainty') is the language of a leader who waits for confirmed intelligence before moving. The previous Ziphite betrayal had taught David that the network of informants includes both friends of Saul and (he hopes) friends of his own. He uses his own scouts to crosscheck. Faith in this period does not preclude due diligence; the man whose protection is the LORD's still sends out scouts. God's people are not asked to be naive about the world; only to act within it under His final sovereignty.

And David arose, and came to the place where Saul had pitched: and David beheld the place where Saul lay, and Abner the son of Ner, the captain of his host: and Saul lay in the trench, and the people pitched round about him. Guidance: David moves close enough to scout the camp visually. He sees the layout: Saul in the center 'in the trench' (the maagal, a circular wagon-barricade or earth berm around the king as the innermost defensive ring); Abner the commander beside him; the rank-and-file ringed around them in concentric circles. The picture is the standard ancient encampment formation: high-value asset in the middle, layered protection radiating outward. The narrator is preparing us for the dramatic infiltration. To get to Saul, David and a companion will have to penetrate every layer of the encampment, sleep-walking past hundreds of armed men, reach the innermost ring, and exit again without being detected. The verse is a setup; it tells us what David is about to defy.

Then answered David and said to Ahimelech the Hittite, and to Abishai the son of Zeruiah, brother to Joab, saying, Who will go down with me to Saul to the camp? And Abishai said, I will go down with thee. Guidance: Two volunteers in the room — Ahimelech the Hittite (the only mention of him in scripture; a foreigner who has joined David's band) and Abishai son of Zeruiah, David's nephew and Joab's brother. The name 'Abishai' will become central in the rest of David's story; he is one of the three most dangerous warriors David ever commands, eventually credited with killing three hundred men with a spear (2 Sam 23:18). Both are willing; only one is chosen. Abishai's answer — 'I will go down with thee' — is the friend-volunteer pattern from 14:7 ('Behold, I am with thee according to thy heart'). The detail about Abishai's family ('brother to Joab') foreshadows the next decades: Abishai and Joab will be the muscle of David's reign and also the source of many of its moral problems (they will assassinate Abner; Joab will eventually kill Absalom). Tonight, though, Abishai is the one who climbs into the wagon-circle with the LORD's anointed and learns what restraint looks like.

So David and Abishai came to the people by night: and, behold, Saul lay sleeping within the trench, and his spear stuck in the ground at his bolster: but Abner and the people lay round about him. Guidance: The two men infiltrate the camp at night. The image is iconic: Saul sleeping inside the wagon-circle, his spear (the same spear he has thrown at David three times) stuck point-down in the ground beside his head — the standard royal sleep arrangement, weapon within instant reach. Abner lies beside him, the people around. Everyone is asleep. David and Abishai stand over the king of Israel. Two men with swords in the middle of an army of three thousand, looking down at the man who has been hunting them for years. The vulnerability is total. The same king who has used the spear as the instrument of attempted murder is now lying defenseless beside it. The reversal is so complete that the temptation to act on it must be enormous. Every soldier's instinct, every personal-grievance instinct, every kingdom-arithmetic instinct says: do it now. The chapter's whole moral weight is going to depend on whether David's restraint can hold against an opportunity even more dramatic than the cave at En-gedi.

Then said Abishai to David, God hath delivered thine enemy into thine hand this day: now therefore let me smite him, I pray thee, with the spear even to the earth at once, and I will not smite him the second time. Guidance: Abishai whispers the proposal. The theological framing — 'God hath delivered thine enemy into thine hand' — is identical to what David's men said at En-gedi (24:4), and identical to what Saul had said about David at Keilah (23:7). The phrase has become the loyal follower's standard interpretation of opportunity. Abishai even volunteers to do the killing himself, sparing David the act — 'let me smite him.' And he promises competence: 'I will not smite him the second time.' One thrust, clean and total, no need for a second blow. The grim efficiency is the killing of someone competent in the dark. Abishai is offering David the perfect plausible-deniability assassination: the king dies in his sleep, by another man's hand, in a remote encampment, with no witnesses, and David's hands stay clean. The temptation has been engineered with precision. And David refuses.

And David said to Abishai, Destroy him not: for who can stretch forth his hand against the LORD's anointed, and be guiltless? David said furthermore, As the LORD liveth, the LORD shall smite him; or his day shall come to die; or he shall descend into battle, and perish. The LORD forbid that I should stretch forth mine hand against the LORD's anointed: but, I pray thee, take thou now the spear that is at his bolster, and the cruse of water, and let us go. Guidance: David's response is the most extended theology of restraint in the wilderness narrative. (1) 'Destroy him not.' Direct command, no softening. (2) The principle: 'who can stretch forth his hand against the LORD's anointed, and be guiltless?' The anointing is a category that survives the anointed person's moral failure. Saul is no longer worthy of his office; he is still in it until God removes him. (3) The three ways Saul might die that do not require David's hand: 'the LORD shall smite him' (sovereign intervention), 'his day shall come to die' (natural death), 'he shall descend into battle, and perish' (combat death). David lists all three so Abishai understands David has thought about this. There are exactly three theologically clean exit-strategies for Saul, and assassination by David's hand is none of them. (4) The alternative action: take the spear and the cruse of water. The spear is the most personal of all of Saul's symbols — the instrument of his attempted murders, his constant companion, the king's identifying accessory. The cruse of water beside it is the most intimate of trail-camp items — the water a sleeping man might wake at night to sip. David takes both. He will walk out with the king's signature weapon and the king's bedside water and leave the king alive. The trophy is the proof of what restraint chose to do.

So David took the spear and the cruse of water from Saul's bolster; and they gat them away, and no man saw it, nor knew it, neither awaked: for they were all asleep; because a deep sleep from the LORD was fallen upon them. Guidance: The most explicit divine intervention in the chapter is reserved for a sentence about sleep. 'A deep sleep from the LORD was fallen upon them.' The word for 'deep sleep' (tardemah) is the same word used of the sleep on Adam in Genesis 2:21 when God took his rib, and on Abraham in Genesis 15:12 when the LORD made covenant. It is a divinely-induced unconsciousness, not ordinary tiredness. The LORD has put the entire camp — three thousand soldiers, the commander, the king — into a sleep so deep that two men walk through them carrying the king's belongings and no one stirs. The protective providence is comically thorough. David's restraint is being honored by the LORD; the same God who could have struck Saul down anesthetizes him so David doesn't have to. The miracle is the kind that goes unwitnessed — no one in Saul's camp will ever know how close to losing their king they came. God's most powerful interventions are sometimes never reported in the official accounts. They happen in the dark, on the perimeter, while the men who would have suffered are sleeping the sleep of the unaware.

Then David went over to the other side, and stood on the top of an hill afar off; a great space being between them: And David cried to the people, and to Abner the son of Ner, saying, Answerest thou not, Abner? Then Abner answered and said, Who art thou that criest to the king? Guidance: David and Abishai cross the valley and climb the opposite slope. They get to safe distance — 'a great space being between them' — before David speaks. He chooses Abner, the captain of the host, as his addressee. The choice is calculated. Abner is the man personally responsible for the king's safety; the man who slept beside Saul without waking; the man whose competence Saul has been relying on. David's voice across the valley summons Abner like a courtroom call. 'Answerest thou not, Abner?' — the rebuke is delivered as a question. Abner's reply — 'Who art thou that criest to the king?' — is the verse-detail that tells us the camp is now awake. (The deep sleep has lifted; David made it across the perimeter under cover of it, and now uses the morning awakening to put the question on the record.) Abner does not yet know it is David; he assumes some madman is shouting from across the valley.

Abner does not yet know it is David; he assumes some madman is shouting from across the valley.

And David said to Abner, Art not thou a valiant man? and who is like to thee in Israel? wherefore then hast thou not kept thy lord the king? for there came one of the people in to destroy the king thy lord. This thing is not good that thou hast done. As the LORD liveth, ye are worthy to die, because ye have not kept your master, the LORD's anointed. And now see where the king's spear is, and the cruse of water that was at his bolster. Guidance: David's address to Abner is brutal and sardonic. The opening flatter — 'art not thou a valiant man? who is like to thee in Israel?' — is the very phrase used of David himself in 18:14 ('all Israel and Judah loved David, because he behaved himself wisely'), now turned ironically against the man whose job was to be the kingdom's chief warrior. Then the indictment: 'wherefore then hast thou not kept thy lord the king?' Then the announcement: 'there came one of the people in to destroy the king thy lord.' (David, with grim wit, refers to himself as a generic infiltrator — Abner does not realize until later that the infiltrator is the very man Abner has been hunting.) The conclusion: 'ye are worthy to die, because ye have not kept your master, the LORD's anointed.' Note: David applies the same phrase ('the LORD's anointed') to Saul that he uses to justify his own restraint. The very office Abner is supposed to protect is the same office that has kept Saul alive tonight. David is teaching Abner Saul's own theological vocabulary. And the proof of David's accusation: 'see where the king's spear is, and the cruse of water.' Pointing at the empty space beside Saul's head and the trophies in David's hand. The argument is total and incontestable.

And Saul knew David's voice, and said, Is this thy voice, my son David? And David said, It is my voice, my lord, O king. And he said, Wherefore doth my lord thus pursue after his servant? for what have I done? or what evil is in mine hand? Guidance: Saul recognizes David's voice — the same recognition scene as at En-gedi (24:16). 'Is this thy voice, my son David?' The 'my son' returns, the recognition tender for a moment. David's reply is courtly: 'It is my voice, my lord, O king.' He could have said many other things; he opens with the polite formula of a subject to a sovereign. Then his question, the same question he asked across the valley at En-gedi: 'wherefore doth my lord thus pursue after his servant? what have I done? or what evil is in mine hand?' He is forcing Saul, again, to confront the absence of any actual crime. The pursuit has no juridical basis; Saul has had several chapters to produce one and never has. Sometimes the most powerful weapon a wronged person has is the unembellished question: what exactly are you accusing me of doing?

And he said, Wherefore doth my lord thus pursue after his servant? for what have I done? or what evil is in mine hand? Now therefore, I pray thee, let my lord the king hear the words of his servant. If the LORD have stirred thee up against me, let him accept an offering: but if they be the children of men, cursed be they before the LORD; for they have driven me out this day from abiding in the inheritance of the LORD, saying, Go, serve other gods. Guidance: One of David's most theologically rich speeches. He proposes two diagnostic possibilities and a remedy for each. (1) If the LORD has stirred Saul up against David — for some sin in David that David is unaware of — then 'let him accept an offering.' A sacrifice will atone, and the matter can be settled with God. (2) But if 'children of men' (human informants — the Ziphites, Doeg's tribe, the courtiers) have stirred Saul up, then 'cursed be they before the LORD; for they have driven me out this day from abiding in the inheritance of the LORD.' The phrase 'the inheritance of the LORD' is the technical Hebrew term for the land of Israel itself — the territory God gave to the tribes as their portion. By driving David out of the land, the informants are functionally telling him to go live among foreigners — 'saying, Go, serve other gods.' The theology is striking: leaving the land is, by default, leaving the worship-community of the LORD. The Old Testament covenant tied worship of the LORD to the land geography of Israel; outside the land, foreign gods dominated public religious life. David is naming a profound spiritual cost of his exile that has nothing to do with politics: it is keeping him from the worship infrastructure that nourishes his soul. The complaint will be repeated in the Psalms of the wilderness years — 'my soul is among lions… O when shall I appear before God?' Sometimes the deepest wound of unjust pursuit is not the physical danger but the spiritual isolation it forces.

Now therefore, let not my blood fall to the earth before the face of the LORD: for the king of Israel is come out to seek a flea, as when one doth hunt a partridge in the mountains. Guidance: David closes with a plea and an image. 'Let not my blood fall to the earth before the face of the LORD' — the image is of the LORD seeing innocent blood spilled on the ground, the very thing that cries out to Him for vengeance throughout scripture (Abel, Naboth, the prophets). David is asking Saul not to make God witness a murder. Then the self-image: 'the king of Israel is come out to seek a flea, as when one doth hunt a partridge in the mountains.' The flea image reprises the one from chapter 24 — Saul's pursuit is disproportionate to its target. The partridge image is new and arrestingly accurate: the partridge is hunted by being chased across the rocky hillside until exhaustion makes it slow enough to catch. The hunt does not catch the bird with one strike; it wears the bird down over hours. David is describing exactly his own experience of these wilderness years. He has not been killed; he has been worn down. The image evokes a particular kind of cruelty — the kind that does not kill quickly but exhausts gradually. Saul is not just hunting; he is hunting in the slow-attritional style that makes the pursuit itself a form of suffering. The flea and the partridge — small, slight, sustained — are David's self-portraits before Saul's three thousand soldiers.

Then said Saul, I have sinned: return, my son David: for I will no more do thee harm, because my soul was precious in thine eyes this day: behold, I have played the fool, and have erred exceedingly. Guidance: Saul confesses. 'I have sinned.' The same words he said in chapter 15 (after Amalek) and in chapter 24 (after the cave). Three times now Saul has said 'I have sinned' and none of the three has produced lasting change. The repetition is itself the diagnosis. 'Return, my son David: for I will no more do thee harm.' Saul invites David back, promises to stop hurting him. The promise will be broken; David will not return. The most telling phrase is Saul's self-assessment: 'I have played the fool (sakal), and have erred exceedingly.' The verb sakal is one root letter different from the recurring word for David's wisdom (sakal — 'behaved wisely'). Saul names himself with the inverse of the word that describes David. He has now publicly identified himself as the fool of the story. The recognition is honest; the change will not come. Words of confession that are not followed by a change in behavior are the tragic genre Saul has perfected. His honesty about himself does not produce his repentance from himself.

And David answered and said, Behold the king's spear! and let one of the young men come over and fetch it. The LORD render to every man his righteousness and his faithfulness: for the LORD delivered thee into my hand to day, but I would not stretch forth mine hand against the LORD's anointed. And, behold, as thy life was much set by this day in mine eyes, so let my life be much set by in the eyes of the LORD, and let him deliver me out of all tribulation. Guidance: David does not accept Saul's invitation to return. He returns the spear instead. 'Behold the king's spear! Let one of the young men come over and fetch it.' He treats it as a transaction across the valley — keep your distance, send a runner, take back what is yours. The reconciliation does not bridge the distance. Then the prayer that becomes the chapter's spiritual center: 'The LORD render to every man his righteousness and his faithfulness.' David asks God to be the judge of both men. He has just demonstrated his own faithfulness; he leaves it to God to render to him whatever that demonstration deserves. Then the closing line — one of the great prayers of the wilderness years: 'as thy life was much set by this day in mine eyes, so let my life be much set by in the eyes of the LORD.' The logic is exquisite: I have valued your life today; may God value mine in His. The same God who watches what one man does to another also watches what God Himself does with the one who acted rightly. The prayer is for proportional mercy — that David's choice tonight be the kind of choice God remembers when David needs deliverance later. The prayer will be answered. God does remember.

Then Saul said to David, Blessed be thou, my son David: thou shalt both do great things, and also shalt still prevail. So David went his way, and Saul returned to his place. Guidance: Saul speaks his last words to David. 'Blessed be thou, my son David: thou shalt both do great things, and also shalt still prevail.' The blessing is sincere within its moment. Saul names David's future king-shaped destiny: 'thou shalt do great things, and also shalt still prevail' (i.e., not just attempt great things but actually succeed at them). It is the most generous prophetic word Saul ever speaks. The two men, who have been bound by anointing and broken by jealousy, will never speak again. 'So David went his way, and Saul returned to his place.' The chapter's last sentence is the geographical proof that the relationship is over. They turn and walk in opposite directions, never to share another conversation. We will see them once more in the same chapter — chapter 28, the night of the witch of Endor — but they will not communicate; the Saul who summons Samuel's ghost is no longer the Saul who blessed David from a hillside. The wilderness years are ending. The next chapters will accelerate toward Saul's death on Gilboa and David's accession at Hebron. David will spend a year and four months among the Philistines as a buffer against Saul; Saul will lose his last battle and his life; the kingdom will pass. But this hilltop blessing is the last positive note in their relationship — the king of Israel acknowledging, in his most lucid moment, that the next king of Israel is the man across the valley with the king's spear in his hand. Sometimes the most generous thing a leader does in his decline is name the person who will replace him, and bless the replacement on the way out the door. Saul does it once, and then goes home to die.
